        🖥️
        System Hardware
        
      

      
    

    

      
Reading hardware…

    

  







  


    

      

        ⓘ
        

          
Add Capacity — Guide

          
Understand what happens before you add disks

        

      

      
    


    


      
      

        

          RAIDZ Expansion
          ZFS 2.3+ only · 1 disk
        

        

          The selected disk is attached directly into your existing RAIDZ-1 or RAIDZ-2 vdev,
          increasing its capacity in-place. No new vdev is created — the pool's fault tolerance is fully maintained throughout
          the process. ZFS rebalances data in the background after expansion.


          Why reported capacity may temporarily decrease:
          During expansion ZFS must rewrite existing data blocks to account for the wider stripe. Until rebalancing completes,
          ZFS reports the pool's usable capacity conservatively — it accounts for the worst-case space overhead of
          the in-progress rewrite. This is expected behaviour: reported capacity will rise progressively as each segment
          finishes rebalancing, returning to (and exceeding) the pre-expansion size once the process is complete.
          Pool data remains fully accessible and protected throughout.


          ⚠ Only available when your pool's top-level vdev is RAIDZ-1 or RAIDZ-2 and ZFS ≥ 2.3 is installed.
          Not available for mirror or stripe pools.
        

      


      
      

        

          Adding a New Vdev
          Mirror · RAIDZ-1 · RAIDZ-2 · Stripe
        

        

          A completely new, independent vdev is appended to the pool. ZFS stripes data across all vdevs —
          so capacity grows immediately. However, each vdev is independent: if the new vdev fails entirely,
          only the data on that vdev is lost (the rest of the pool survives).
        


        
        

          

            
💾

            

              
Mirror (min 2 disks)

              
All disks in the vdev are identical copies. Best read performance. Usable capacity = size of one disk × number of pairs.

            

          

          

            
💾

            

              
RAIDZ-1 (min 3 disks · RAID5)

              
Single distributed parity. Survives 1 disk failure. Usable = (n−1) × disk size.

            

          

          

            
💾

            

              
RAIDZ-2 (min 4 disks · RAID6)

              
Double distributed parity. Survives 2 simultaneous failures. Best protection for large-disk arrays.

            

          

          

            
💾

            

              
Stripe (any number of disks)

              
No redundancy within the vdev. Maximum usable space. Any disk failure in this vdev loses all data in it.

            

          

        

      


      
      

        ⚠ Important: Adding a new vdev is permanent — vdevs cannot be removed from a pool once added.
        All selected disks will have their existing data erased. Make sure you have selected the right disks before confirming.
      


    


    

      
    

  







  


    
    

      

        ⓘ
        

          
ZFS Pool Layout Guide

          
This decision is permanent — choose carefully

        

      

      
    


    
    

      ⚠
      Pool layout cannot be changed after creation. Migrating to a different layout requires destroying the pool and losing all data.
    


    
    


      
      

        

          

            Stripe
            No redundancy
          

          Min 1 disk · 100% usable
        

        
        

          
💾

          
💾

          
💾

          → all capacity combined, no protection
        

        

          Maximum throughput and usable capacity. All disks form a single volume.
          One disk failure destroys the entire pool and all data.
          Suitable only for scratch space or data you can afford to lose.
        

      


      
      

        

          

            RAIDZ
            mirror
          

          Min 2 disks · ~50% usable
        

        
        

          

            
💾

            ⇄
            
💾

            mirror pair
          

          + add more pairs to scale capacity
        

        

          Every disk is fully duplicated on its mirror partner. Survives 1 disk failure per pair.
          Excellent read performance. Capacity = half the total raw disk space.
          You can add more mirror pairs later to grow the pool.
        

      


      
      

        

          

            RAID-10
            stripe of mirrors
          

          Min 4 disks (even) · 50% usable
        

        
        

          

            
💾

            ⇄
            
💾

          

          +
          

            
💾

            ⇄
            
💾

          

          → pairs striped together
        

        

          Multiple 2-way mirrors striped into a single pool. Each pair survives 1 disk failure.
          Reads scale across all disks; writes scale across pairs. Capacity = half the total raw disk space.
          Best balance of speed and resilience for VM/database workloads. Disk count must be even.
        

      


      
      

        

          

            RAIDZ-1
            RAID5
          

          Min 3 disks · (n−1)/n usable
        

        
        

          
💾

          
💾

          
💾

          
P

          1 parity block distributed across disks
        

        

          Single distributed parity. Survives 1 disk failure. With 3 disks: ⅔ usable capacity; with 4 disks: ¾.
          Good balance of capacity and protection. Rebuild time increases with disk size.
          Requires ZFS 2.3+ for online disk expansion within the vdev.
        

      


      
      

        

          

            RAIDZ-2
            RAID6
          

          Min 4 disks · (n−2)/n usable
        

        
        

          
💾

          
💾

          
💾

          
P

          
P

          2 parity blocks
        

        

          Double distributed parity. Survives any 2 simultaneous disk failures — critical for arrays of 6+ disks
          where the probability of a second failure during rebuild is non-negligible.
          Most suitable for large drives (8 TB+) where rebuild windows are long.
        

      


    


    
    

      
    

  







  

    

      
⚠ Delete Encryption Key

      
    

    

      

      

        You are about to permanently delete the key .
      

      

        The key file will be removed from the server. Any pool or dataset encrypted with this key will become inaccessible unless you have an exported backup of the key.
      

      
This action cannot be undone.

Add InterLink Server

      
    

    

      

      

        
        
      

      

        
        
      

      

        
        
      

    

    

      
      
    

  







  

    

      
Remote 2FA Required

      
    

    

      

        The admin account on the remote server has two-factor authentication enabled.
        Enter the 2FA code to complete linking.
      

      

        
        
      

    

    

      
      
    

  







  

    

      
Push to Remote Server via InterLink

      
    

    

      


      
      

        📁
        

          
Source

          

        

      


      
      

        
        
      


      
      

        
        
      


      
      

        
        
      


      
      

        
        
        

          A temporary snapshot will be created locally, sent to the remote server via zfs send, then deleted automatically. Progress is shown in the activity bar.
        

      


      
      

        

          

            
Source size

            

          

          
→

          

            
Pool available

            

          

        

        

      

    

    

      
      
    

  







  

    

      
Push Snapshot via InterLink

      
    

    

      

      

        
        

      

      

        
        
      

      

        
        
      

      

        
        
        

          The first push does a full ZFS send. Subsequent pushes of the same snapshot base will be incremental.

Switching to systemd Networking

      
    

    

      
Live migration log:

      

    

    

      
    

  







  

    

      
Configure Static IP

      
    

    

      

        Virtualization rewrites your network onto a bridge (vmbr0). A DHCP address changes during that conversion and again when the bridge comes up, so the server can end up on a new IP. Pinning a static address first avoids the churn.
        


        The fields below are pre-filled with your current address — keep them as-is to reserve the IP you already have (no disruption), or edit them if you want a different static address.
      

      

      

        
        
      

      

        
        
      

      

        
        
      

      

        

          
          
        

        

          
          
        

      

    

    

      
      
    

  







  

    

      
⚠ Cannot shrink memory compression now

      
    

    

      
Lowering the zram pool requires draining the data it currently holds back into physical RAM (or disk swap), and there isn't enough free space to do that safely right now.

      

        
Currently in zram:
–

        
RAM + disk-swap free:
–

        
Requested new size:
–

      

      
You can:

      
    
        
  • Free memory and retry — stop or shrink a VM/container, then click Apply again. Cancel below to keep the new value staged in the form.
    • 
        
  • Apply on next reboot — save the new percentage to /etc/default/zramswap now; the live device keeps its current size until the host reboots.
    •

⚠ Confirm Downgrade

      
    

    

      

        You are about to downgrade ZFSNAS to .
      

      

        This will replace the running binary and restart the portal. Ensure the older version is compatible with your current configuration.
